data.source: Laboratory experiment using moral dilemma vignettes and lexical decision task
data.notes: Participants were Korean college students recruited for a mixed-design experiment; random assignment to observed vs. control groups.

method.description: Participants first completed a baseline moral-dilemma pretest, then were randomly assigned to an Observed (performance ostensibly monitored by two experimenters) or Control condition and responded to 50 new dilemmas (20 personal, 15 impersonal, 15 non-moral). Afterward they performed a lexical decision task and filled out personality questionnaires. Behavioural data (choices, decision confidence, reaction time) were analysed with mixed-design ANOVAs.
steps: Recruit and screen 48 healthy Korean college students.
Measure current mood and arousal ratings.
Administer pretest with 20 process-dissociation moral dilemmas to obtain baseline D and U parameters.
Randomly assign participants to Observed (OBS) or Control (CON) group; implement social-observation manipulation.
Administer main task: 50 trolley-type dilemmas; record binary moral choice, confidence rating, reaction time.
Administer lexical decision Go/No-Go task with Reputation, Warmth, Competence, and control words.
Collect post-experiment questionnaires (IRI, Need for Cognition, Fear of Negative Evaluation) and repeat mood/arousal ratings.
Analyse data with SPSS 23.0: mixed ANOVAs (Group × Dilemma Type etc.), t-tests, correlation analyses.
method.models: Mixed-design ANOVA, paired and independent samples t-tests, Process Dissociation analysis (Jacoby 1991)
method.outcome_variable: Proportion of deontological judgments (primary); also decision confidence, reaction time, lexical decision RTs
method.independent_variables: Group (Observed vs Control), Dilemma type (personal, impersonal, non-moral), Response type (appropriate/utilitarian vs inappropriate/deontological)
method.control_variables: Baseline moral orientation (D parameter), personality traits (empathy, need for cognition, fear of negative evaluation), sex, response-key counterbalancing
method.tools_software: Cogent 2000 toolbox in MATLAB 8.4 for task presentation; SPSS 23.0 for statistics; G*Power 3.1 for power analysis

results.summary: Social observation led to a higher proportion of deontological choices specifically in personal moral dilemmas, without baseline trait differences between groups. Observation also lowered overall decision confidence and lengthened reaction times. Lexical decision data showed faster responses to warmth-related words and slower responses to competence words under observation, indicating activation of warmth-signalling goals.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Proportion of deontological judgments in personal moral dilemmas (OBS vs CON)
Overall reaction time across moral dilemmas (OBS − CON difference)
Interaction Group × Dilemma Type on deontological judgments

metadata.original_paper_title: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas
